questions.stem: A Banach space is defined as which kind of space?
questions.explanation: A Banach space is a normed vector space that is complete with respect to the metric induced by its norm. Completeness, not finite dimension or compactness, is the defining extra property over a general normed space.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: A normed space is complete when which condition holds?
options: Every Cauchy sequence converges to a limit in the space.
Every sequence has a bounded subsequence.
questions.explanation: Completeness means every Cauchy sequence has a limit lying inside the space. This rules out missing limit points (gaps). Bounded sequences need not converge even in a Banach space.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Is $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the Euclidean norm a Banach space?
options: No, it is only a normed space.
Only the closed unit ball is complete.
Yes; it is a complete normed space.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the Euclidean (indeed any) norm is complete: a Cauchy sequence is Cauchy coordinatewise, and each coordinate converges in the complete space $\mathbb{R}$. Hence $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a Banach space for every $n$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Is the space $C[a,b]$ of continuous functions with the supremum norm a Banach space?
options: Yes; a uniformly Cauchy sequence of continuous functions converges uniformly to a continuous limit.
No, because continuous functions need not be bounded.
Only the polynomials in it are complete.
It is complete only for the integral norm.
questions.explanation: Under the sup norm, a Cauchy sequence in $C[a,b]$ is uniformly Cauchy, hence converges uniformly; the uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous, so the limit stays in $C[a,b]$. Thus $C[a,b]$ with the sup norm is a Banach space.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: For $1 \le p < \infty$, the sequence space $\ell^p$ consists of scalar sequences $(x_n)$ satisfying which condition?
questions.explanation: By definition $\ell^p = \{ (x_n) : \sum_n |x_n|^p < \infty \}$, with norm $\|x\|_p = ( \sum_n |x_n|^p )^{1/p}$. Boundedness alone defines $\ell^\infty$, and $x_n \to 0$ defines $c_0$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Is the sequence space $\ell^p$ (for $1 \le p < \infty$) with its natural norm complete?
questions.explanation: Each $\ell^p$ with $1 \le p \le \infty$ is complete, hence a Banach space. (For $p = 2$ it is moreover a Hilbert space.) The proof shows a Cauchy sequence converges coordinatewise to a limit that still lies in $\ell^p$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Is the space $\mathbb{Q}$ of rationals, with the absolute value norm, complete?
options: Yes, every normed space over $\mathbb{Q}$ is complete.
It is complete only for bounded sequences.
No; a rational sequence approaching $\sqrt{2}$ is Cauchy but has no rational limit.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Q}$ is not complete: truncated decimal approximations of $\sqrt{2}$ form a Cauchy sequence of rationals whose limit $\sqrt{2}$ is irrational. Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$ does not give completeness; it is exactly the failure of completeness that $\mathbb{R}$ repairs.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: A normed space $X$ is a Banach space if and only if which series condition holds?
options: Every absolutely convergent series in $X$ converges in $X$.
Every series with terms tending to zero converges.
questions.explanation: A normed space is complete exactly when every absolutely convergent series (one with $\sum_n \|x_n\| < \infty$) is convergent in the space. This is a standard characterization of completeness via series and is often used to prove a space is Banach.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: In a Banach space, does absolute convergence of a series $\sum_n x_n$ (that is, $\sum_n \|x_n\| < \infty$) imply convergence of $\sum_n x_n$?
options: No, the implication fails in every infinite-dimensional space.
Yes; in a Banach space absolute convergence implies convergence.
Only if the terms are orthogonal.
questions.explanation: Completeness guarantees that absolute convergence implies convergence: the partial sums form a Cauchy sequence, which converges because the space is complete. This holds in every Banach space, including infinite-dimensional ones.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: A subspace $M$ of a Banach space $X$ is itself a Banach space (in the restricted norm) if and only if which holds?
questions.explanation: A subspace of a complete space is complete exactly when it is closed: a Cauchy sequence in $M$ converges in $X$, and the limit lies in $M$ for all such sequences iff $M$ contains its limit points. Finite-dimensional subspaces are automatically closed and hence complete.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Is every finite-dimensional normed space complete?
options: No, completeness can fail in low dimensions.
Only those with the Euclidean norm.
Yes; every finite-dimensional normed space is a Banach space.
Only those over the complex field.
questions.explanation: Every finite-dimensional normed space is complete. Since all norms on a finite-dimensional space are equivalent to the Euclidean norm, and that space is complete, the original space is too. Thus finite-dimensional normed spaces are always Banach spaces.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Consider $C[0,1]$ with the integral ($L^1$) norm $\|f\| = \int_0^1 |f(t)|\, dt$ instead of the sup norm. Is it complete?
options: Yes, every norm on $C[0,1]$ is complete.
No; a Cauchy sequence can converge in the $L^1$ sense to a discontinuous function outside $C[0,1]$.
questions.explanation: Under the $L^1$ norm, $C[0,1]$ is not complete. Continuous functions that increasingly approximate a step function are Cauchy in the integral norm, but their natural limit is discontinuous and so lies outside $C[0,1]$. Completeness of $C[0,1]$ depends on using the sup norm.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Convergence in the supremum norm on $C[a,b]$ is the same as which mode of convergence?
questions.explanation: Since $\|f_n - f\|_\infty = \max_t |f_n(t) - f(t)|$, convergence in the sup norm means $\sup_t |f_n(t) - f(t)| \to 0$, which is precisely uniform convergence. This is why the uniform limit of continuous functions staying continuous makes $C[a,b]$ complete.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: The completion of a normed space $X$ is a Banach space $\widehat{X}$ in which $X$ sits how?
options: As the whole space, since $X$ is already complete.
questions.explanation: Every normed space $X$ has a completion $\widehat{X}$, a Banach space containing an isometric copy of $X$ as a dense subspace. The completion is unique up to isometric isomorphism. For example $\mathbb{R}$ is the completion of $\mathbb{Q}$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Which sequence space is the standard example of a normed space that is NOT complete?
options: The space $c_{00}$ of finitely supported sequences with the sup norm.
questions.explanation: The space $c_{00}$ of eventually-zero sequences, under the sup norm, is not complete: the sequence whose $n$-th member is $(1, 1/2, \dots, 1/n, 0, 0, \dots)$ is Cauchy but its natural limit $(1, 1/2, 1/3, \dots)$ has infinitely many nonzero terms and so lies outside $c_{00}$. The other three spaces are all complete.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Is the space $\ell^\infty$ of bounded scalar sequences, with the sup norm, a Banach space?
options: Yes; $\ell^\infty$ with the sup norm is complete.
No, bounded sequences cannot form a complete space.
Only its convergent sequences form a Banach space.
Only when the scalars are real.
questions.explanation: $\ell^\infty$ with $\|x\|_\infty = \sup_n |x_n|$ is complete. A Cauchy sequence in $\ell^\infty$ converges coordinatewise to a bounded limit, and the convergence is uniform in the coordinate index, so the limit lies in $\ell^\infty$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: What distinguishes a Banach space from a Hilbert space?
options: A Banach space need not be complete.
A Hilbert space is a Banach space whose norm comes from an inner product.
A Hilbert space need not be complete.
questions.explanation: A Hilbert space is a complete inner product space, i.e. a Banach space whose norm satisfies the parallelogram law and so arises from an inner product. Every Hilbert space is a Banach space, but not conversely (for instance $C[a,b]$ with the sup norm).
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Which statement about the relationship between Cauchy and convergent sequences is true in a general normed space (not assumed complete)?
options: Every convergent sequence is Cauchy, but some Cauchy sequences may fail to converge.
Convergent sequences need not be Cauchy.
questions.explanation: In any normed space convergent implies Cauchy. The converse, that every Cauchy sequence converges, is exactly completeness and can fail in an incomplete space such as $\mathbb{Q}$ or $c_{00}$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: The space $c_0$ of scalar sequences converging to $0$, with the sup norm, has which property?
options: It is not a vector space.
It is a closed subspace of $\ell^\infty$, hence a Banach space.
questions.explanation: $c_0$ is a closed subspace of the Banach space $\ell^\infty$: a uniform limit of sequences tending to zero again tends to zero. A closed subspace of a Banach space is complete, so $c_0$ with the sup norm is itself a Banach space.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: A necessary condition for a series $\sum_n x_n$ to converge in a Banach space is which of the following?
questions.explanation: If $\sum_n x_n$ converges then its partial sums are Cauchy, forcing the individual terms $x_n = s_n - s_{n-1} \to 0$ in norm. This is necessary but not sufficient (the harmonic series shows $\|x_n\| \to 0$ does not guarantee convergence). Absolute convergence is sufficient, not necessary.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Which of the following spaces is NOT complete (with the indicated norm)?
options: The polynomials on $[0,1]$ with the sup norm.
questions.explanation: The space of polynomials with the sup norm is not complete: by the Weierstrass theorem polynomials are dense in $C[0,1]$, so a sequence of polynomials can converge uniformly to a continuous non-polynomial (such as $e^t$), which lies outside the space. The other three are standard Banach spaces.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Why is completeness of a normed space such a useful hypothesis in analysis?
options: It guarantees limits of Cauchy sequences exist in the space, so constructive and iterative arguments stay inside it.
It forces every bounded set to be compact.
It makes every linear operator bounded.
questions.explanation: Completeness ensures that Cauchy sequences (produced by approximation, iteration, or series) actually converge to elements of the space. This underlies fixed point arguments, the Baire-category-based theorems, and the convergence of absolutely convergent series. It does not imply compactness of bounded sets in infinite dimensions.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: In an infinite-dimensional Banach space, is the closed unit ball $\{ x : \|x\| \le 1 \}$ compact?
options: Yes, the closed unit ball is always compact.
Yes, because it is closed and bounded.
No; by Riesz's lemma the closed unit ball is compact only in finite dimensions.
It is compact only in Hilbert spaces.
questions.explanation: Riesz's lemma shows the closed unit ball of a normed space is compact if and only if the space is finite-dimensional. In infinite dimensions one can build a sequence of unit vectors that stay a fixed distance apart, so no convergent subsequence exists. Closed and bounded does not imply compact beyond finite dimensions.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Is the space $C^1[a,b]$ of continuously differentiable functions complete under the plain sup norm $\|f\|_\infty = \max_t |f(t)|$?
options: Yes, because differentiable functions are continuous.
Yes, the sup norm makes every function space complete.
It is complete only on a bounded interval.
No; a uniformly convergent sequence of $C^1$ functions can have a non-differentiable limit.
questions.explanation: Under the sup norm alone, $C^1[a,b]$ is not complete: smooth functions can converge uniformly to a continuous but non-differentiable function (uniform convergence does not preserve differentiability). To make $C^1$ a Banach space one uses the stronger norm $\|f\|_\infty + \|f'\|_\infty$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Classify these spaces (each with its standard norm) as complete or not: $\mathbb{R}^n$ (Euclidean), $C[a,b]$ (sup norm), $\ell^2$, and $\mathbb{Q}$ (absolute value). Then count the complete ones.
questions.solution: The spaces $\mathbb{R}^n$, $C[a,b]$ with the sup norm, and $\ell^2$ are all complete, while $\mathbb{Q}$ is not. Among these four spaces, the number that are complete is {{1}}. The space $\mathbb{Q}$ {{2}} complete, because a rational sequence approaching $\sqrt{2}$ is Cauchy without a rational limit. A complete normed space is called a {{3}} space.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{R}^n$, $C[a,b]$ (sup norm), and $\ell^2$ are complete ($3$ of the four); $\mathbb{Q}$ is not. A complete normed space is a Banach space.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Use the series characterization of completeness. Let $X$ be a normed space.
questions.solution: The space $X$ is complete if and only if every absolutely convergent series in $X$ {{1}}. In particular, in a Banach space, if $\sum_n \|x_n\|$ converges then $\sum_n x_n$ {{2}}. A necessary condition for any series $\sum_n x_n$ to converge is that the norms $\|x_n\|$ tend to {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Completeness is equivalent to every absolutely convergent series being convergent. So in a Banach space absolute convergence implies convergence. For any convergent series the terms must satisfy $\|x_n\| \to 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Reason about $C[0,1]$ under two different norms.
questions.solution: Under the supremum norm, $C[0,1]$ {{1}} complete, since a uniformly Cauchy sequence of continuous functions has a continuous uniform limit. Under the integral ($L^1$) norm, $C[0,1]$ {{2}} complete, because continuous approximations to a step function are Cauchy but the limit is discontinuous. Convergence in the supremum norm is exactly {{3}} convergence.
questions.explanation: With the sup norm $C[0,1]$ is complete (uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous). With the $L^1$ norm it is not complete. Sup-norm convergence is uniform convergence.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis

questions.stem: Recall when a subspace of a Banach space is itself a Banach space, and the standard sequence-space facts.
questions.solution: A subspace $M$ of a Banach space is itself complete if and only if $M$ is {{1}} in the ambient space. The sequence space $\ell^p$ for $1 \le p \le \infty$ {{2}} complete. The number of the spaces $\ell^1$, $\ell^2$, $\ell^\infty$ that are Banach spaces is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: A subspace of a Banach space is complete exactly when it is closed. Every $\ell^p$ with $1 \le p \le \infty$ is complete, so all three of $\ell^1, \ell^2, \ell^\infty$ are Banach spaces.
questions.source: in the style of Kreyszig, Introductory Functional Analysis
